diagrams.title: TestAndSet Atomic Operation Flow
diagrams.description: Shows the step-by-step execution of the TestAndSet instruction as an atomic operation
elements: Rectangle box labeled 'TestAndSet(boolean *target)'
Arrow from 'Entry' to 'Read *target' rectangle
Rectangle showing 'boolean original = *target' (read current value)
Arrow to rectangle showing '*target = true' (unconditional write)
Arrow to rectangle showing 'return original' (return old value)
Arrow pointing back to 'Exit'
Circular arrow around all steps labeled 'ATOMIC OPERATION (No Interruption'
Note: All steps execute as single uninterruptible unit
diagrams.purpose: Students understand that TestAndSet executes atomically without context switching, making it suitable for implementing mutual exclusion in concurrent systems

diagrams.title: Spinlock Implementation using TestAndSet
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing how TestAndSet creates a mutual exclusion lock mechanism
elements: Two boxes side by side: 'Lock Variable (boolean lock = false)'
Arrow pointing from Lock to 'TestAndSet Function' box
TestAndSet box shows code: while(TestAndSet(&lock));
Arrow from function to 'Critical Section' box (only entered when lock was false)
Arrow from Critical Section points to 'lock = false' (release lock)
Dotted loop arrow from release back to Lock
Additional boxes showing 'Waiting Process' loop when lock was true
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how spinlocks work - processes busy-wait (spin) until the lock becomes available, using TestAndSet for atomic lock acquisition

diagrams.title: TestAndSet vs Compare-and-Swap Comparison
diagrams.description: Comparison block diagram of two hardware synchronization primitives
elements: Left side: 'TestAndSet' box with characteristics: Single boolean, returns old value, sets to true
Right side: 'Compare-and-Swap' box with characteristics: Takes expected and new values, atomic compare and swap, returns success/failure
Arrows from both boxes pointing to 'Used for Mutual Exclusion'
Arrows pointing to 'Used for Lock-free Data Structures'
Bottom section shows code comparison: TAS: atomic swap, CAS: conditional swap
diagrams.purpose: Students understand different hardware primitives for synchronization and their use cases in modern concurrent programming

diagrams.title: Process Synchronization with TestAndSet
diagrams.description: Timeline diagram showing two processes competing for a lock using TestAndSet
elements: Horizontal timeline with Process P1 (top) and Process P2 (bottom)
Time axis showing: T0, T1, T2, T3, T4, T5
At T0: Both processes call lock()
At T1: P1 executes TestAndSet, gets 'false', sets lock='true', enters critical section
At T1 parallel: P2 executes TestAndSet, gets 'true' (lock already true), loops back waiting
At T2-T3: P1 in critical section
At T4: P1 exits critical section, sets lock='false'
At T4 parallel: P2 executes TestAndSet, gets 'false', enters critical section
Vertical dotted lines showing atomic execution windows
Color coding: Green=lock acquired, Red=waiting/spinning
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize the race condition resolution and understand how TestAndSet ensures only one process enters critical section at a time

charts.title: Lock Acquisition Flowchart
charts.content: Flowchart showing decision path: Start → Call TestAndSet → Check returned value → If false (lock was free): Enter critical section, exit loop → If true (lock was busy): Continue spinning → End. Shows busy-wait loop behavior of spinlocks using TestAndSet.

charts.title: Synchronization Methods Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table: TestAndSet (hardware atomic instruction, busy-wait, simple, good for short critical sections) vs Compare-and-Swap (more flexible, lock-free programming, complex) vs Semaphores (OS-supported, blocking, no busy-wait) vs Mutex (high-level, blocking, portable). Columns: Method, Type, Wait Behavior, Complexity, Use Case.

charts.title: TestAndSet Operation Truth Table
charts.content: Shows all possible states: Input (lock value) | Output (returned original) | New lock value | Process enters critical section? | Cases: false → false (enter), true → true (wait). Demonstrates the atomic nature and mutual exclusion guarantee.

charts.title: Types of Mutual Exclusion Mechanisms
charts.content: Classification: Software-based (Dekker's, Peterson's) → Pure software solutions; Hardware-based (TestAndSet, Compare-and-Swap, Swap instruction) → Atomic instructions; OS-supported (Semaphores, Monitors, Mutex) → Kernel-level. TestAndSet falls under Hardware-based category.

branches: Purpose: Mutual Exclusion (one process in critical section)
Result: Returns old value, sets lock to true
Use Case: Spinlocks for process synchronization
Advantages: Simple, atomic, no OS kernel involvement
Platform Support: x86 (XCHG instruction), ARM, SPARC
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting TestAndSet to its role in operating system process synchronization, showing its position among synchronization primitives and its practical applications in implementing mutual exclusion.

branches: TestAndSet: Atomic boolean swap, busy-wait spinlock
Critical Section: Code segment accessing shared resources
Mutual Exclusion: Only one process in critical section at a time
Progress: No process waits indefinitely if section is free
Bounded Waiting: Upper limit on times other processes enter after waiting
OS Primitives: Semaphores, Monitors, Condition Variables
Practical Application: Operating system kernel locks, multi-threaded applications
mindMaps.purpose: Shows how TestAndSet fits into the broader topic of process synchronization in operating systems, connecting the atomic instruction to the fundamental problem of mutual exclusion that all concurrent systems must solve.
